entries.title: Interweaving Micro and Macro - Tool usage in FPS (by EOO)
entries.summary: Rewiring the Use of Tools such as Aim Trainers to push Micro heavy training into a function of your macro economic gameplay is a concept that has become widely lost, the push of raw aim , raw mechanics, 1v1 isolation has become a popular side niche in the aim training improvement and progress community.
entries.body:
Rewiring the Use of Tools such as Aim Trainers to push Micro heavy training into a function of your macro economic gameplay is a concept that has become widely lost, the push of raw aim , raw mechanics, 1v1 isolation has become a popular side niche in the aim training improvement and progress community.

The concept of interweaving heavily trained micro into your macro game play in your PvP environment has become overlooked as well as looked down upon or straight up ignored completely. I feel this has become a detriment to the progress of developing well rounded individuals that excel and push the aim training community into a direction that is not only looked down upon but further encompasses the whole subsections of mechanic improvers into sharing ideas ,concepts and blending communities in this space. 

Macroeconomic gameplay is not only vital but fundamental in your improvement as a player and as an mechanics enjoyer, once you can understand how to combine both this will not only push you as a player it will push you into understanding how training macro is just as important as micro as its also just as translatable into every environment/sandbox you put yourself in regarding pushing micro, for example macro is being utilised in the aim trainer sense where you are adjust sens to cheese certain scenario’s this is a macro function being used to increase your micro performance.

When you look at how the community treats aim trainers, it's almost always framed as a purely micro tool, just mastering mouse control and pushing pb’s and rotating playlists/benchmarks  , but there's a whole macro layer baked into that process that nobody talks about. Every time you adjust your sens, your FOV, your scenario settings to target a specific weakness, that's a macro decision shaping a micro outcome. That distinction matters because it teaches you improvement is about decision making, knowing what reps to even run and why, not just grinding volume. People who just grind raw clicking scenarios without ever asking what specific transferable skill it maps to in their actual PvP gameplay are leaving half the equation on the table. 

Macro is the translation layer, it's the part of your brain reading the fight, positioning, cooldowns, teamfight economy, deciding when and how to even deploy the micro you've built. Skip training that layer and you get players who can smoke a bot scenario at insane accuracy but freeze up or make garbage reads the second real variables and unpredictability show up, cuz they've never practiced applying micro through a macro lens. 

Thats why the sens cheesing example hits so well, its a perfect microcosm of the whole idea. Adjusting sensitivity for a scenario is you using macro level thinking, figuring out what setting lets you isolate and overload a specific mechanic, to accelerate a micro gain. You could argue that a truly great scenario is one that you cant cheese but now you are introducing developments to the structure of the sandbox in your pursuit to push raw training to a fair level, all this does it push the niche boundary to an even more niche that truly is far from what we want to develop in the first place.
That same skill, zooming out and asking what adjustment right now gets me the outcome I want, is the exact neural pathway that's firing when a player adjusts positioning, target priority, or engagement timing mid-fight. It's the same discipline expressed at different scales. Once people start seeing it that way, real well rounded improvement starts happening instead of just grinding numbers that never transfer. 

Join our discord: https://discord.com/invite/5UfdER6YAK

entries.title: All The Movement Resources You'd Need
entries.body:
For starters, the biggest thing I'd recommend is reading Sam or @400apm's github page (https://bysam.github.io/strafe/). A good summary of this page is How using positioning in coordination with your strafe is so much more beneficial than just moving without intention.



Next is aimer7's strafe aim guide. Available here (https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/k239yqdivljka2cc6p535/Strafe-Aiming-101.pdf?rlkey=ah0qn4g0kq7sx90bh6gy764f4&st=bibs0gzc&dl=0), it's one of the best resources available for movement aim and, while complex, is used to this day by many mechanics coaches as not only a reference but a bible of sorts.
